5 min read
Just now

--

Photo by Tolu Akinyemi 🇳🇬 on UnsplashModern software doesn’t stay online because one powerful server never fails.
It stays online because many smaller systems work together, constantly protecting one another.
Let’s build that architecture.
Building a System That Refuses to Go Offline
Imagine you’re opening your first restaurant.
Initially, one chef, one cashier, and one waiter are enough.
Business is manageable.
But after a year, your restaurant becomes famous.
Hundreds of customers arrive every hour.
Would you still depend on just one chef?
Probably not.
Because if that chef falls sick, the entire restaurant stops.
Software systems face exactly the same challenge.
Instead of relying on a single server, engineers distribute responsibilities across multiple machines.
A typical High Availability architecture looks something like this:
🌍 Internet Users
│
▼
DNS Server
│
▼
Global Load Balancer
┌─────────┴─────────┐
▼ ▼
Availability Zone A Availability Zone B
│ │
┌──────┴──────┐ ┌──────┴──────┐
▼ ▼ ▼ ▼
App Server 1 App Server 2 App Server 3 App Server 4
│ │ │ │
└─────────────┼─────┼─────────────┘
▼
Redis Cache
│
▼
Primary Database ←→ Read Replica
│
▼
Backup & Object Storage
│
▼
Monitoring • Logging • AlertingNotice something interesting.
There isn’t a single component responsible for availability.
Instead, every layer protects another layer.
If one application server crashes…
The Load Balancer simply removes it from rotation.
Users continue using the application without even realizing something failed.
That’s the beauty of High Availability.

The Unsung Hero: Load Balancer
Imagine arriving at a toll plaza with ten lanes.
Would it make sense for every vehicle to queue behind a single booth?
Of course not.
Traffic would become unbearable.
Instead, vehicles are distributed across multiple counters.
Load Balancers perform exactly the same job.
Every incoming request first reaches the Load Balancer.
It decides which healthy server should handle that request.
If one server becomes overloaded…
Traffic shifts elsewhere.
If one server crashes…
It stops receiving requests altogether.
Popular production Load Balancers include:

• NGINX

• HAProxy

• Traefik

• AWS Application Load Balancer (ALB)

• Azure Application Gateway

• Google Cloud Load Balancer
Without a Load Balancer, one failed server could make your entire application unavailable.

Health Checks: The Silent Doctor
How does a Load Balancer know whether a server is healthy?
It doesn’t guess.
Every few seconds, it performs a Health Check.
Think about your annual medical check-up.
Your doctor measures blood pressure, heart rate, and oxygen levels before declaring you healthy.
Servers undergo similar check-ups.
A Health Check might ask:

• Is the application responding?

• Is the database connection working?

• Is memory usage within limits?

• Is the API returning HTTP 200?
If the answer is “No”…
The server is temporarily removed from service.
Users are automatically redirected elsewhere.
No engineer has to wake up at 2 AM to manually reroute traffic.
Automation keeps the system available.

One Data Center Is Never Enough
Suppose your application is hosted in a single data center.
Everything works perfectly.
Then suddenly…
A power outage affects the building.
Or a network fiber is accidentally cut.
Or severe flooding damages the facility.
No matter how powerful your servers are…
They’re all unavailable.
That’s why cloud providers introduced Availability Zones (AZs).
Think of an Availability Zone as an independent mini data center.
Each one has:

• Separate power

• Separate cooling

• Separate networking
If one zone experiences problems…
Another zone continues serving users.
For even greater resilience, companies deploy across multiple regions.
For example:

• Mumbai

• Singapore

• Frankfurt

• Virginia
If an entire city becomes unavailable, traffic automatically moves to another region.
This is how companies like Google, Microsoft, and Amazon achieve global availability.

Cloud Providers Make High Availability Easier
Years ago, companies had to purchase physical servers, networking equipment, backup generators, and storage.
Today, cloud providers offer many of these capabilities as managed services.
AWS

• Elastic Load Balancer

• Auto Scaling Groups

• Multi-AZ RDS

• Route 53 Health Checks

• Amazon CloudWatch
Microsoft Azure

• Azure Load Balancer

• Availability Zones

• Azure Traffic Manager

• Azure Monitor

• Virtual Machine Scale Sets
Google Cloud

• Cloud Load Balancing

• Managed Instance Groups

• Cloud Monitoring

• Cloud DNS

• Regional & Global Deployments
If you’re building with open-source technologies, similar capabilities are available using:

• Kubernetes

• NGINX

• HAProxy

• Keepalived

• Prometheus

• Grafana

• Loki
Cloud platforms don’t eliminate outages.
They simply provide the building blocks to design highly available systems more easily.

Availability Always Comes with Trade-offs
If someone tells you,
“Let’s build a system with 100% uptime.”
Be cautious.
Nothing in engineering is free.
Higher availability always increases cost and complexity.
Architects constantly ask:
“How much downtime can the business actually tolerate?”
A banking platform may justify Five Nines.
A small internal HR portal probably doesn’t need it.
Good architecture is always driven by business requirements — not by chasing impressive numbers.

What Interviewers Usually Ask
When Availability appears in a System Design interview, interviewers rarely ask for definitions.
Instead, they explore your thought process.
Typical questions include:

• What is High Availability?

• Explain the difference between Availability and Reliability.

• What does 99.99% uptime actually mean?

• How would you eliminate a single point of failure?

• Why do companies use multiple Availability Zones?

• Explain Active-Active and Active-Passive architectures.

• How would you design a highly available e-commerce platform?
A senior engineer doesn’t begin by saying,
“Let’s add more servers.”
They first ask,
“What level of availability does the business require?”
That’s the architectural mindset.

What Changed My Thinking Today?
Before learning System Availability, I believed keeping software online simply meant buying a bigger server.
Today I understand that availability is the result of thoughtful architecture — not powerful hardware.
It’s about removing single points of failure.
Designing automatic recovery.
Planning for disasters before they happen.
And most importantly…
Understanding that users don’t care why your application went down.
They only remember that it did.

Up Next: Day 7 — Maintainability
Imagine inheriting a 15-year-old application with millions of lines of code, no documentation, and dozens of developers afraid to touch it.
How do companies like Google, Spotify, and Netflix continue evolving massive systems without breaking everything?
In the next article, we’ll explore Maintainability — why clean architecture, modular design, observability, documentation, and engineering culture are just as important as writing good code.
Because great software isn’t only built to scale.
It’s built to evolve.
